coredata.dc:title: One-step hydrothermal preparation of a novel 2D MXene-based composite electrode material synergistically modified by CuS and carbon dots for supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yuchun
Liu, Jiachen
Gong, Tianle
Liang, Changzhi
Li, Lin
Lin, Xuemei
Ying, Zongrong
Liu, Huangyan
coredata.dc:description: 2D material MXene is one of the most interesting electrode materials due to its vast range of potential applications in electrochemical energy storage and conversion in recent years. Herein we fabricated a novel 2D MXene-based composite electrode material by using hydrothermally synthetized CuS and carbon dots (CDs) to synergistically decorate Ti3C2Tx. The introduction of CuS and CDs increases the interlayer spacing of Ti3C2Tx, which reduces the stacking of Ti3C2Tx unfavorable to the electrochemical performance. The optimal C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS composite electrode has an excellent specific capacitance of 1186 F·g−1 at a current density of 1 A·g−1, which is much higher than that of Ti3C2Tx samples decorated with single CuS or with single CDs, illustrating that CuS and CDs have the outstanding synergistic improvement effect to MXene on the electrochemical performance. When the current density rises to 10 A·g−1, the sample still maintains a capacitance of 907 F·g−1, corresponding to a capacitance retention rate of 76.5%. After 1000 cycles at a current density of 1 A·g−1, the capacitance remains 67% of the initial value. These demonstrate that the C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS composite electrode material has a great promising practical potential in fabricating high-performance supercapacitors. The new finding about the outstanding synergistic effect of CDs and transition metal compounds on improving MXene’s electrochemical performance suggests a possible effective way for preparing the high-performance MXene-based electrode materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MXene
Ti3C2Tx
CuS
Carbon dot
Synergistic effect
Supercapacitor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme illustration of the synthesis route to the C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS composites.
(a) XRD patterns of all samples and (b) the corresponding enlarged XRD patterns of the Ti3C2Tx (002) peaks for C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-m (m=1, 2, 3, or 4) samples.
SEM images of Ti3C2Tx (a, b), Ti3C2Tx/CuS (c, d), C-Ti3C2Tx (e, f) and C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 (g, h).
HRTEM images (a-g) and SAED pattern (h) of C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2.
HRTEM image and the corresponding EDS mapping images and spectrum of the C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2.
High resolution XPS spectra of C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2: (a) Full spectra, (b) Cu 2p, (c) S 2p, (d) C 1 s and (e) Ti 2p.

(a) CV curves of Ti3C2Tx, Ti3C2Tx/CuS, C-Ti3C2Tx and C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 electrodes at a scan rate of 50 mV·s−1; (b) CV curves of four C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-m (m=1, 2, 3, or 4) electrodes at a scan rate of 50 mV·s−1; (c) GCD curves of all electrodes at a current density of 1 A·g−1; (d) Specific capacitance values of all electrodes at a current density of 1 A·g−1; (e) Nyquist impedance plots for all electrodes; and (f) Nyquist impedance plots of four C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-m (m=1, 2, 3, or 4) electrodes in the magnified high-frequency region.

(a) CV curves of C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 at different scan rates; (b) GCD curves of C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 at various current densities; (c) Specific capacitance of four C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-m (m=1, 2, 3, or 4) varying with the current densities; (d) Cycling performance of the C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 electrode with an inset showing the GCD curves of the C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 electrode before and after 1000 cycles at 1 A·g−1.
Comparison of electrochemical performance with similar reported systems.

row.entry: MXene based vertical aligned MoS2 nanosheets

head.ce:title: One-step hydrothermal preparation of a novel 2D MXene-based composite electrode material synergistically modified by CuS and carbon dots for supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Soft Chemistry and Functional Materials of Education Ministry, Department of Materials Science and Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Soft Chemistry and Functional Materials of Education Ministry, Department of Materials Science and Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Soft Chemistry and Functional Materials of Education Ministry, Department of Materials Science and Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 2D material MXene is one of the most interesting electrode materials due to its vast range of potential applications in electrochemical energy storage and conversion in recent years. Herein we fabricated a novel 2D MXene-based composite electrode material by using hydrothermally synthetized CuS and carbon dots (CDs) to synergistically decorate Ti3C2Tx. The introduction of CuS and CDs increases the interlayer spacing of Ti3C2Tx, which reduces the stacking of Ti3C2Tx unfavorable to the electrochemical performance. The optimal C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS composite electrode has an excellent specific capacitance of 1186 F·g−1 at a current density of 1 A·g−1, which is much higher than that of Ti3C2Tx samples decorated with single CuS or with single CDs, illustrating that CuS and CDs have the outstanding synergistic improvement effect to MXene on the electrochemical performance. When the current density rises to 10 A·g−1, the sample still maintains a capacitance of 907 F·g−1, corresponding to a capacitance retention rate of 76.5%. After 1000 cycles at a current density of 1 A·g−1, the capacitance remains 67% of the initial value. These demonstrate that the C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS composite electrode material has a great promising practical potential in fabricating high-performance supercapacitors. The new finding about the outstanding synergistic effect of CDs and transition metal compounds on improving MXene’s electrochemical performance suggests a possible effective way for preparing the high-performance MXene-based electrode materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The emerging need for high-power energy storage and conversion devices in many application fields has led to lots of researches on various high-performance electrode materials and their assembled devices [1–5]. As novel energy storage devices, supercapacitors have two kinds of energy storage principle: electric double layer and pseudocapacitance [6,7]. Carbon materials like mesoporous carbon, carbon nanotubes and graphene are typical electric-double-layer electrode materials, which feature long electrochemical stability but poor energy density [8,9]. Compared to electric-double-layer electrode materials, pseudocapacitive materials, such as transition metal oxides [4], hydroxides [10,11], sulphides [12–15] and conductive polymers [16,17], have higher specific capacitance and energy density, thus becoming the most promising electrode materials.

Recently, with good electrical conductivity, good hydrophilicity and unique stacking structure, MXene becomes an emerging 2D material with a promising application prospect in energy storage [6,15–22]. Various forms of MXene-based electrode materials have widely been explored in recent years [2,23], such as nanospheres [24] , films [25,26], porous bodies [27,28], aerogels [12,29], hydrogels [1], and micro-supercapacitors [30] . The related researches mainly involve two aspects. One is on the structure control [31–35], doping optimization [36,37] and functionalization [38,39] of single MXenes, and the other is on the hybrid composite of MXenes with other electrochemical active compounds [40–42] like carbon [5,22], carbides [43] , transition metal oxides [4,20,21,24], hydroxides [11,12,44,45], selenids [7], metal-organic frameworks [46–48], and conductive polymers [16,17,49]. Transition metal sulfides are considered to be very useful electrode materials [18,19] due to high energy density and specific capacitance, thus the MXene-based composites with various sulfides including CuS [15,18,50], NiS [18] , MoS2 [19,51,52], WS [53] , CoNi2S4 [54] , NiMoS4 [55] and so on have been focused on.

Carbon (quantum) dots (CDs), with the most dimensions of less than 20 nm, graphite-like or amorphous carbon structure, and rich oxygen-containing functional group, have attracted some attention from researchers in the energy storage field in recent years [56–58], with only a few reports about MXene combining with CDs [58].

Herein, a novel 2D MXene-based composite electrode material was prepared by simultaneously synthetizing CuS and CDs onto Ti3C2Tx matrix only via a simple and low-cost one-step hydrothermal method with the aim of exploring the synergistical effect of CDs with CuS on improving the electrochemical properties of MXene. The target product C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS shows outstanding capacitance performance with a specific capacitance of 1186 F·g−1 at a current density of 1 A·g−1, higher than that of most recently-reported MXene-based materials. Most surprised, single CuS or CDs shows very weak improvement to MXene in capacitance performance, but their combination has outstanding modification effect on MXene. Such outstanding synergistic effect is first found, which may shed light on designing high-performance MXene-based electrodes for various energy storage/conversion devices.

Cu(NO3)2·3 H2O, HCl and Na2S were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. LiF and KOH were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Cooperation. Glucose was supplied by Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ti3AlC2 was provided by Jilin 11 technology Co., Ltd. Acetylene black, polytetrafluoroethylene emulsion and nickel foam were commercially available. All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

Firstly, 2 g of LiF was added to a polytetrafluoroethylene beaker containing 40 mL of 10 mol L−1 HCl solution and continuously stirred magnetically for 30 min. Then 2 g of Ti3AlC2 was slowly added and reacted at 35 °C in a water bath for 48 h. Their centrifugation product was washed with deionized water until the PH value of the supernatant reached about 7 and next ultrasonicated to be stripped for 1 h. The ultrasonicated dispersion was centrifugated at 3500 rpm for 1 h to obtain Ti3C2Tx dispersion. After being pre-frozen in a refrigerator, the Ti3C2Tx suspension was freeze-dried in a freeze dryer into powder for use.

The sample preparation process is shown in Fig. 1. In a typical process, 30 mg of Ti3C2Tx powder was dispersed into 30 mL of water and sonicated for 1 h. Next, 120 mg of Cu(NO3)2·3 H2O, 1 g of Na2S and 0.05 g, 0.1 g, 0.3 g or 0.5 g of glucose was added into Ti3C2Tx dispersion, and stirred until being dissolved. They were poured into 100 mL of hydrothermal kettle and reacted at 150 ℃ for 24 h. After the reaction, they were naturally cooled to room temperature, and the black precipitate was centrifuged, washed with water and ethanol, and vacuum-dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h. The sample was labeled as C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-1, C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2, C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-3 or C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-4, corresponding to 0.05 g, 0.1 g, 0.3 g or 0.5 g of glucose addition, respectively. For comparison, the sample was prepared with only 0.1 g of glucose or with only 120 mg of Cu(NO3)2·3 H2O and 1 g of Na2S, which was labeled as C-Ti3C2Tx or Ti3C2Tx/CuS, respectively.

Hitachi S-4800Ⅱ field scanning electron microscope (SEM) and Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN field transmission electron microscope (TEM) were employed to observe and photograph the morphology and microstructure. The crystalline structure was characterized by a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos Axis Ultra DLD) was used to determine the chemical bonding states.

The electrochemical performances including cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvano-static charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were measured by using a GHI660C electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Company, China). The long-term cycling stability was examined on the Land battery workstation (LAND CT 2001A, Wuhan Land Instrument Company, China).

For the three-electrode system test, a saturated calomel (Hg/HgO) electrode and a platinum sheet were served as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively, and a 6-mol·L−1 KOH aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte. The working electrode was prepared as below: the finely-grounded sample (around 0.015 g), conductive agent (acetylene black) and binder (polytetrafluoroethylene emulsion) with a mass ratio of 8:1:1 were mixed in a right amount of ethanol to form a homogeneous slurry, and the slurry was evenly coated on one end (1 ×1 cm2) of a clean nickel foam current collector, followed by drying at 60 °C for 12 h and pressed at 10 kN for 1 min to make the active material adhered with the current collector tightly.

The potential range (V) in the GCD test was − 0.8–0.2 V. The mass specific capacitance was calculated from the GCD curves according to the following equation:
C
g
=
I × Δ t
m × Δ V
where
C
g
is the mass specific capacitance of the active material (F·g−1), I is the test current (A), Δt is the discharge time (s), m is the mass of the active material (g), and ΔV is the discharge voltage window excluding IR drop (V).

Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of Ti3C2Tx and its several hydrothermal composites with different composition. For all the samples, strong diffraction peaks were observed at 9.1°, 39.1° and 43.9°, corresponding to the (002), (104) and (105) crystal planes of Ti3C2Tx respectively, revealing the existence of Ti3C2Tx. Found from Fig. 2(a), four samples C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-1, C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2, C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-3 and C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-4 basically have the same peak position and peak shape. Diffraction peaks at 11.1°, 27.9°, 28.9°, 31.7°, 32.9°, 38.6°, 47.9°, 52.0° and 59.3° of 2θ are obvious, corresponding to the (002), (100), (101), (102), (103), (105), (107), (108) and (203) lattice plane, respectively [13,18]. Compared with the standard card JCPDS 06–0464, the conclusions can be drawn that hexagonal CuS exists in all the four samples. The C-Ti3C2Tx sample has an obvious broad envelope peak with extremely weak intensity around 25° attributed to the CDs, while the four C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-m (m=1, 2, 3, or 4) samples have no obvious carbon peak, which is probably because the specific gravity of CDs is relatively low or the hydrothermally-formed CDs become amorphous because of the obstruction of CuS simultaneously produced. Of note, as shown in Fig. 2(b), in addition to the diffraction characteristics of CuS, the Ti3C2Tx (002) peak of these composites has a left-shift phenomenon from 7.10°, which indicates that Ti3C2Tx has a wider interlayer spacing [18,50] because of the hydrothermal intercalation of CuS and CDs.

Fig. 3 presents the morphology of Ti3C2Tx and its three kinds of composites. From Fig. 3(a) and (b), it can be seen that Ti3C2Tx shows scallop-like structure, in which the size of scallop-like body is mainly about several microns. In Fig. 3(c) and (d) for Ti3C2Tx/CuS sample, it can be seen that many irregular CuS particles about 30 nm aggregate together to form various irregular shape, being tightly anchored onto the surface of Ti3C2Tx. Fig. 3(e) and (f) show that within C-Ti3C2Tx sample, there are lots of irregular flocculent bodies attached to the surface of lamellar Ti3C2Tx. It illustrates that carbon substance (i.e., the aggresome of CDs) was hydrothermally synthetized from glucose. Seen in Fig. 3(g) and (h) for C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 sample, some attachments are embedded in the surface of Ti3C2Tx. Considering that the shape of these attachment is not the same as that of pure CuS or that of pure carbon substance, these attachments should be the co-existence of CuS and carbon substance.

Fig. 4 shows the TEM images of the C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 sample. Found from Fig. 4(a) and (b), Ti3C2Tx exists in the form of transparent flakes. In Fig. 4(d) and (e), the lattice fringes of 0.31 nm are ascribed to the (002) crystal plane of CDs [51], in Fig. 4(f), those of 0.26 nm and 0.23 nm are attributed to the (101) and (104) crystal planes of Ti3C2Tx respectively, and in Fig. 4(g), those of 0.281 nm represent the (103) crystal plane of CuS [50]. As seen from Fig. 4(d), the average interlayer spacing of Ti3C2Tx calculated is 1.06 nm, which proves that CuS and CDs successfully insert into between the layer structure of Ti3C2Tx. The (006), (008), (108) and (118) crystal planes of CuS can be observed from the SAED image presented in Fig. 4(h), proving the polycrystalline structure of CuS [50].

The EDS images ( Fig. 5) show the distribution of each element Ti, Cu, S, and C within the C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 sample. We can see from Fig. 5(b) that Ti exists in all the objects observed in Fig. 5(a), demonstrating Ti3C2Tx is the base material embedding CuS and CDs on. From Fig. 5(c) and (d), it can be found that Cu and S present the same distribution shape, proving the existence of CuS and that these shapes belong to CuS. Fig. 5(e) shows the distribution density pattern of C element doesn’t completely correspond to that of Ti element, revealing the presence of CDs.

In order to determine the elemental composition and chemical valence state of the composite, the XPS spectra for C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 sample are shown in Fig. 6 Found from the full spectrum Fig. 6(a), there are Ti, C, Cu, and S elements within C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 composite, suggesting the successful synthesis of CuS and CDs. In Fig. 6(b), there are two peaks at 932.2 eV and 952.0 eV in the Cu 2p region, which are the typical characteristic peaks of Cu2+ [15]. Fig. 6(c) shows four characteristic peaks in the S 2p region, of which one 161.3 eV is for S 2p3/2, one at 162.3 eV for S 2p1/2, one at 163.5 eV for S-M (M=Cu), and one at 168.5 eV for salt [18]. All four peaks can prove that S2- exists in the material, revealing the successful attachment of CuS. The content of S in this sample is high, and the peak strength is closely related to the element content in the sample, so the strengths of S-2p1/2 and S-2p3/2 are relatively strong [50]. The signal of S-2p3/2 is better than that of S-2p1/2, and the peak intensity of fitting curve is higher than that of S-2p1/2. The characteristic peak spectrum of C 1 s is presented in Fig. 6(d), in which the peak at 281.7 eV represents C-Ti originating from Ti3C2Tx, and one at 288.8 eV represents C-O bond [10], the latter along with the C-C characteristic peak of 285.0 eV implying the presence of CDs, which is consistent with the observations in Fig. 5. Fig. 6(e) presents the Ti 2p region, where the Ti 2p3/2 peak at 458.8 eV is the characteristic peak of Ti-C, and the Ti 2p1/2 peak at 464.3 eV is ascribed to Ti-O [45,49], which proves the existence of the Ti3C2Tx. From the XPS spectra, it can be concluded that the material contains many functional groups and electrochemically active sites, which are beneficial to improve the capacity performance and long-term cycling stability.

After understanding the structural and morphological characteristics of the composite electrode materials, the three-electrode system was employed to evaluate their electrochemical performance by using a 6-mol·L−1 KOH solution as the electrolyte. The results are shown in Fig. 7. Fig. 7(a) shows the CV curves of Ti3C2Tx, Ti3C2Tx/CuS, C-Ti3C2Tx, and C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 all exhibit the pseudocapacitance characteristics. The attachment of CuS and CDs to Ti3C2Tx leads to a certain degree of increase in the envelope area, meaning better capacitive property. In fact, Ti3C2Tx/CuS and C-Ti3C2Tx only display a little increase in the envelope area than Ti3C2Tx, revealing that single CuS or single CDs only weakly improves the electrochemical performance of Ti3C2Tx. However, surprisingly, the C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 sample presents much larger envelope area not only than Ti3C2Tx but also than Ti3C2Tx/CuS and C-Ti3C2Tx, clearly illustrating that the co-decoration of CuS and CDs to Ti3C2Tx has notable synergistic effect in enhancing the electrochemical performance, which is apparently rare in the Ti3C2Tx-based electrode materials. Found from Fig. 7(b), the ratio of CuS and CDs anchored to Ti3C2Tx has obvious effects on the electrochemical performance and the C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 sample is optimal. At a scan rate of 50 mV·s−1, these samples containing CuS attachment all have distinct redox peaks, with obvious oxidation peak at the potential of about − 0.2 V and obvious reduction peak at about − 0.5 V, proving that their capacitance is mainly caused by the pseudocapacitance energy storage mechanism. The area of the CV curve at the same scan rate has a positive correlation with the specific capacitance of the electrode material. Among all samples, C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 has the largest enclosing area, indicating the best electrochemical performance.

Furthermore, as presented in Fig. 7(c), at a current density of 1 A·g−1, the samples containing the CuS attachment all exhibit redox plateaus, revealing that C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS has typical pseudocapacitive features. From Fig. 7(c), two basic platues can be observed, which is consistent with the multiple redox peaks observed in Fig. 7(b). The feature of two basic platues is also previously seen in some references [2,45], especially ones about some CuS-based electrode materials for supercapacitors [14,15]. This can be attributed to two probable faradaic reaction processes involved in the C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS composites in alkaline electrolyte. One of the speculated reactions is: CuS+OH-↔CuSOH+e-, Cu0↔Cu++e- [14,15,50]. Pseudocapacitive redox processes also prevail after copper sulfide transforming into hydroxide as illustrated below: CuS+H2O+ 1/2 O2→Cu(OH)2 +S, and Cu(OH)2 + 2 KOH→K2CuO2 + 2 H2O [15] . Based on the GCD curves, the specific capacitances of Ti3C2Tx, C-Ti3C2Tx, Ti3C2Tx/CuS, C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-1, C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2, C-Ti3C2Tx /CuS-3 and C-Ti3C2Tx /CuS-4 are 11.01, 37.12, 196.3, 392.3, 1186, 946.7 and 606.2 F·g−1, respectively, as shown in Fig. 7(d). Obviously, C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-m (m=1, 2, 3, or 4) all present much higher than both that of the Ti3C2Tx/CuS sample decorated only with CuS and that of the C-Ti3C2Tx sample decorated only with CDs, which demonstrates that the co-decoration of CuS and CDs greatly improved the electrochemical performance and implies that the outstanding synergistic effect of CuS and CDs exits. Among four C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-m samples, C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 has the best specific capacitance, which proves the optimal ratio of CDs to CuS results in the most perfect co-modifying micro-nano structures, further showing the most prominent synergistic effect in improving the electrochemical performance. Further, from Fig. 7(c), for four C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-m (m=1, 2, 3, or 4) samples, on their two basic platues some multiple bump-like features can all be observed, which may be attributed to the difference in the degrees of perfection about the micro-nano structures of CuS co-modifying Ti3C2Tx with CDs. Notably, C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 has very weak bump-like features, seemingly revealing that C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 is most perfect in the co-modifying micro-nano structures, which agrees with its best electrochemical performance.

Fig. 7(e) presents EIS plots of all samples in the frequency range from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz, and the EIS plots of C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-m samples in the magnified high-frequency region are by detail displayed in Fig. 7(f). In Fig. 7(e), all the curves are fitted by the equivalent loop method (the experimental errors in fitted parameters is less than 2%) and each curve is composed of a semicircle in the high frequency region and an oblique line in the low frequency region. In the high frequency region, the Rs of the C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 composite is calculated about 1.00 Ω, and the Rct is about 0.85 Ω, indicating that the internal resistance of the active material is small. This also suggests the contact of the electrolyte with the active materials is good, and that the diffusion and transfer of charges in the electrolyte is less hindered when the redox reaction occurs.

Fig. 8 further studies the energy storage performance of the optimal C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 sample. Fig. 8(a) shows the CV curves of C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 at different scan rates. As the scan rate increases, the area enclosed by the CV curve gradually increases. Fig. 8(b) presents the GCD curves at different current density. There all are redox plateaus, further confirming its pseudocapacitive characteristics. From Fig. 8(c), it can be seen that with the current density increasing, the specific capacitance of all the C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-m (m=1, 2, 3, or 4) electrodes decreases. For C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2, when the current density is 1 A·g−1, the specific capacitance reaches up to 1186 F·g−1, higher or much higher than that of most recently-reported Ti3C2Tx-based composite electrode materials (some of them are shown in Table 1). Furthermore, when the current density rises from 1 A·g−1 to 10 A·g−1, the C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 sample still maintains the capacitance of 907 F·g−1, equivalent to a capacitance retention of 76.5%, which proves its excellent rate performance.

To evaluate the long-term cycling performance of the C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 electrode material, GCD experiment was performed at a current density of 1 A·g−1 for 1000 cycles, and the results are illustrated in Fig. 8(d). The cycling properties of electrode materials are crucial for the practical application of supercapacitors. Seen from Fig. 8(d), the specific capacitance decreases in the first 150 cycles, and as the cycling process continues, the specific capacitance tends to be stable. After 1000 cycles at a current density of 1 A·g−1, the sample finally has a retention rate of 67%, indicating that it has good reversibility and cycling life performance, which is due to the perfect and stable co-modifying micro-nano structures of both CuS and CDs within C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2. After a long-term cycling, the GCD curve was tested again, which is exactly the same as the curve before the cycling, implying that the pseudocapacitive performance can be maintained well, as shown in the inset of Fig. 8(d).

A novel 2D MXene-based composite electrode material was prepared by synthetizing CuS and CDs onto Ti3C2Tx matrix with a simple and low-cost one-step hydrothermal method. The introduction of CuS and CDs expanded the interlayer spacing of Ti3C2Tx. The combination of CuS and CDs showed rare notable synergistic effect on enhancing the electrochemical performance to Ti3C2Tx. The optimal C-Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 sample showed a very high specific capacitance of 1186 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, and still presented a specific capacitance of 907 F g−1 at 10 A g−1, reaching a specific capacitance retention of 76.5%. In addition, 67% of the initial capacitance value was maintained after 1000 cycles of charging and discharging processes at a current density of 1 A·g−1. These demonstrate that the Ti3C2Tx/CuS-2 sample is a promising high-performance electrode material for supercapacitors.

Yuchun Li: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Jiachen Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Tianle Gong: Methodology, Investigation. Changzhi Liang: Investigation, Data curation. Lin Li: Methodology. Xuemei Lin: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Zongrong Ying: Supervision. Huangyan Liu: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 4D printing of MXene hydrogels for high-efficiency pseudocapacitive energy storage
Nat. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Li, J. Zhao, A. Zhussupbekova, C.E. Shuck, L. Hughes, Y.Y. Dong, S. Barwich, S. Vaesen, I.V. Shvets, M. Mobius, 4D printing of MXene hydrogels for high-efficiency pseudocapacitive energy storage, Nat. Commun. 13 (2022) 6884.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-performance flexible asymmetric supercapacitor paired with indanthrone@graphene heterojunctions and MXene electrodes
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Xu, B. Pan, W.-S. Li, L. Dong, X. Wang, and F.-G. Zhao, High-performance flexible asymmetric supercapacitor paired with indanthrone@graphene heterojunctions and MXene electrodes, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 13 (2021) 41537–41544.
